title: PHP-FPM max_children Reached and 503 Correlation
dataSources: Counter increments when FPM refuses new accepts — users see 502/503 from the front proxy.
app: Splunk Universal Forwarder; optional Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (`Splunk_TA_nix`); custom `props.conf`/`transforms.conf` for `phpfpm:*` sourcetypes
spl:
index=web sourcetype="phpfpm:status"
| eval mcr=tonumber(max_children_reached)
| where mcr > 0
| timechart span=5m sum(mcr) as max_children_reached_events by pool, host
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable: this use case is not built on a CIM data model. Rely on the search in Step 2 against your raw logs or metrics.
description: The `max children reached` counter increments when FPM cannot spawn workers to accept new connections. It aligns with application errors and proxy connection failures.
value: Triggers immediate scaling or config changes instead of waiting for global outage metrics.
grandmaExplanation: We use this to triggers immediate scaling or config changes instead of waiting for global outage metrics.
implementation: Alert on any `max_children_reached` increase. Correlate with `phpfpm:access` 5xx and front-end nginx/haproxy errors.
visualization: Time series (pool utilization, queue, latency percentiles), single value alerts, top charts for scripts and methods.
knownFalsePositives: Thread or worker pools fill during traffic spikes, slow upstream services, or DoS-style load. A surge alone can be benign if backends recover.
